It is a brilliant detail that completely changes how you read the text.
The treatise explains that Hebrew poetry does not rely
on rhyming words at the end of a line, the way a lot of traditional English poetry does.
Instead, it relies heavily on rhythm and meter.
And there is a very specific meter used for elegies and laments.
It is called the Kena meter.
Yes, Kena simply translates to a lament or a cry of deep sorrow.
And the mechanics of this meter are fascinating.
It uses a long, short rhythm technically described as a 3.2 beat.
We need to explain how that feels to you, the listener.
Why use a 3.2 beat for a sad song?
The scholars in the
treatise suggest that the 3.2 beat mimics the actual physical rhythm of a human being sobbing.
Think about the physical response of crying deeply.
You take a long, ragged breath in and then you let out a short, sharp exhale or a sob.
A long breath in, a short breath out.
Three beats, then two beats.
The first line of the couplet is longer, carrying three stresses.
The second line is cut short with only two stresses.
The poetry physically forces the reader to breathe
in the rhythm of a person who is weeping.
The second line always feels unresolved, like a life that has been suddenly cut short.
